**Open-Data Credit Risk Early-Warning System for US Non-Financial Firms (2010-2025)**

**Executive summary**

This proposal outlines an MSc-level project to build an **interpretable Credit Risk Early-Warning System (EWS)** for **US listed, non-financial firms** using **open-source public data only**. The EWS outputs a **monthly probability score** that a firm will experience **credit deterioration within the next 12 months**, plus a **risk trajectory** suitable for analyst monitoring (watchlists and escalation triage), not trading.

The project is grounded in canonical distress-prediction and credit-risk literature-**Altman (1968)** ratio-based discrimination, **Ohlson (1980)** probabilistic logit modeling, **Shumway (2001)** discrete-time hazard modeling with market and accounting predictors, and **Merton (1974)** structural credit risk intuition (optional distance-to-default proxy).

Data will be collected from **SEC EDGAR** (JSON APIs for submissions history and extracted XBRL facts), **Yahoo Finance via the open-source** yfinance **library** for daily equity prices, and **FRED** for macro/stress indicators (including VIX). VIX interpretation is justified using **official Cboe methodology**.

To ensure feasibility under time and team constraints, the analysis focuses on **~60-80 firms** over **2010-2025**, at **monthly frequency** (≈ 80 × 192 ≈ 15,360 firm-month observations, plus daily raw prices for rolling features). The modeling stack prioritizes interpretability: pooled logistic regression (baseline), fixed-effects panel logit, and discrete-time hazard logit. Validation uses **time-based splits and rolling windows**, reporting AUROC, AUPRC, Brier score, top-K lift/capture, and lead-time.

**Research background and motivation**

Credit deterioration is rarely a single-day event; it is typically a **process** reflected in weakening fundamentals (leverage/liquidity/profitability) and forward-looking market stress (drawdowns, volatility, rising funding stress). A practical challenge is that many core accounting signals are updated only quarterly or annually; therefore, an early-warning system should incorporate higher-frequency signals that can capture adverse changes **between** reporting dates.

The academic foundations for building an interpretable EWS are well established. Altman (1968) shows that combinations of financial ratios can discriminate bankrupt from non-bankrupt firms, forming a classical benchmark for distress prediction. Ohlson (1980) casts failure prediction in a probabilistic logit framework, aligning naturally with a modern "probability score" output. Shumway (2001) argues that single-period models can be inconsistent for forecasting bankruptcy and proposes a discrete-time hazard approach-estimable via logit-that explicitly handles time variation and integrates market-driven predictors (returns/volatility) with accounting ratios.

To strengthen the theoretical justification for market-based warning signals, the project optionally leverages the structural intuition in Merton (1974), where default relates to firm asset value dropping below promised debt obligations; this motivates a simplified distance-to-default style proxy as an additional interpretable signal (optional under time constraints).

A key motivation of this project is **reproducibility with open data**. The SEC provides documented access to EDGAR data via JSON APIs (submissions history and extracted XBRL facts), enabling a transparent pipeline without paid databases.

**Problem definition and research objectives**

**Problem definition**

![](data:image/png;base64,)

The system must support **early warning**, meaning evaluation emphasizes whether risk increases **before** deterioration and whether the model concentrates future deteriorations in the highest-risk bucket (top-K lift/capture).

**Research objectives**

The project has five objectives:

First, build a monthly firm-level panel (2010-2025) combining SEC fundamentals, market stress measures, filing-based warning signals, and macro controls using open data.
Second, define implementable deterioration labels suitable for open-data constraints.
Third, estimate interpretable models (pooled logit, fixed-effects panel logit, discrete-time hazard logit), aligned with classic literature and MSc rigor.
Fourth, evaluate with time-based splits and rolling windows using early-warning metrics.  
Fifth, present interpretable outputs (coefficients, marginal effects, calibration, and risk-decile monitoring).

**Scope and market selection**

**Scope: inclusions**

The empirical setting is **US listed non-financial firms** observed monthly from 2010 to 2025 (month-end). Industry grouping uses **SIC codes** as disclosed in EDGAR filings; the SEC explicitly states that SIC codes in disseminated EDGAR filings indicate a company's type of business and are used for review assignment, making them defensible for filtering and fixed effects.

Data sources are restricted to open/public sources: SEC EDGAR APIs and filings, Yahoo Finance via yfinance, and FRED macro series; VIX interpretation is anchored in Cboe methodology.

**Scope: exclusions**

The project excludes (i) paid/proprietary datasets, (ii) deep learning/black-box methods, and (iii) production deployment. It also excludes financial firms and (optionally) utilities, because regulated/financial balance sheets can make ratio comparability weaker; utility exclusion will be treated as a robustness check (assumption).

**Market and industry selection rationale**

The US is chosen primarily for **data accessibility and reproducibility**: the SEC documents that submissions history and extracted XBRL data are available via RESTful JSON APIs on the SEC data platform.

**Sample size and sampling strategy**

**Assumption:** Sample size is intentionally reduced to **~60-80 firms** for feasibility given team/time constraints.

Sampling strategy (implementable, reproducible):

- Start from SEC-published ticker/CIK mapping files (used to map tickers to unique firm identifiers).
- Retrieve each candidate's SIC code from EDGAR metadata and exclude financials (and optionally utilities) based on SIC filters.
- Require minimum data coverage (assumption): at least 8 years of daily prices and at least 8 quarters of fundamentals to avoid sparse panels.
- Stratify across broad SIC groups (e.g., 2-digit SIC) to avoid sector concentration; sample proportionally to reach ~60-80 firms.

**Data description and label design**

**Data sources and how to obtain them**

**SEC EDGAR fundamentals and filing metadata (primary/official).**  
The SEC's EDGAR APIs provide (i) submissions history by company and (ii) extracted XBRL data in JSON. The SEC developer resources page explicitly highlights that submissions by company and extracted XBRL data are available via RESTful APIs with JSON output.
Operational constraints: SEC guidance emphasizes fair access and rate control; the SEC states a current maximum request rate of 10 requests/second and reserves the right to limit request rates.

**Equity market data (Yahoo Finance via** yfinance**).**  
yfinance is open source and documents that it is not affiliated with Yahoo; it uses Yahoo's publicly available APIs and is intended for research/educational use, and users should refer to Yahoo's terms for data usage rights.

**Macro and stress indicators (FRED + Cboe).**  
FRED web services require API keys for requests, and provide series observation endpoints for programmatic download.
VIX is obtained from the FRED series VIXCLS, whose notes state it measures market expectation of near-term volatility conveyed by stock index option prices.
Cboe's VIX methodology document states VIX measures 30-day expected volatility of the S&P 500 and uses SPX option prices and treasury yield curve rates as inputs.

**Filing-based "signals" (SEC forms).**  
Two filing types are used as signals and event benchmarks: Form 8-K Item 1.03 (Bankruptcy or Receivership) and Form 12b-25 (Notification of Late Filing).

**Time horizon, frequency, and dataset size**

- Time horizon: 2010-2025 (inclusive), monthly.
- Sample: ~60-80 firms (assumption).
- Panel size: 200 firm-month observations (80 × 192), excluding the last 12 months where forward labels cannot be computed.

**Deterioration labels (exact definitions)**

The project uses two labels to balance statistical power and event precision.

**Label A (primary): market-implied deterioration.**  
![](data:image/png;base64,)

Threshold choice (−40%) is an explicit design decision; robustness checks will test alternative thresholds (−30%, −50%) to assess sensitivity (assumption).

**Label B (secondary/high-precision): bankruptcy disclosure.**  
Define deterioration if an SEC Form 8-K is filed under Item 1.03 "Bankruptcy or Receivership" within the next 12 months (event benchmark). The SEC's Form 8-K explicitly contains Item 1.03 requirements.
Implementation assumption: item detection is done by parsing the filing text/headers for Item 1.03 identifiers; the mapping will be validated on a small hand-checked subset to control false positives.

**Variables/features table**

**Table 1. Variables and features (minimal, interpretable set)**

| **Feature group** | **Variable / feature**        | **Source**                 | **Native frequency** | **Panel frequency** | **Calculation / notes**                                          |
| ----------------- | ----------------------------- | -------------------------- | -------------------- | ------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Fundamentals      | Total assets (TA)             | SEC XBRL (companyfacts)    | Q/A                  | Monthly             | Last available as of (t), carry-forward by filing date           |
| ---               | ---                           | ---                        | ---                  | ---                 | ---                                                              |
| Fundamentals      | Total liabilities (TL)        | SEC XBRL                   | Q/A                  | Monthly             | Carry-forward                                                    |
| ---               | ---                           | ---                        | ---                  | ---                 | ---                                                              |
| Fundamentals      | Cash & equivalents            | SEC XBRL                   | Q/A                  | Monthly             | Carry-forward                                                    |
| ---               | ---                           | ---                        | ---                  | ---                 | ---                                                              |
| Fundamentals      | Current assets (CA)           | SEC XBRL                   | Q/A                  | Monthly             | Carry-forward                                                    |
| ---               | ---                           | ---                        | ---                  | ---                 | ---                                                              |
| Fundamentals      | Current liabilities (CL)      | SEC XBRL                   | Q/A                  | Monthly             | Carry-forward                                                    |
| ---               | ---                           | ---                        | ---                  | ---                 | ---                                                              |
| Fundamentals      | Operating income (EBIT proxy) | SEC XBRL                   | Q/A                  | Monthly             | TTM if feasible; else annualized last quarter (assumption)       |
| ---               | ---                           | ---                        | ---                  | ---                 | ---                                                              |
| Ratios            | Leverage                      | Derived                    | -                    | Monthly             | (TL/TA)                                                          |
| ---               | ---                           | ---                        | ---                  | ---                 | ---                                                              |
| Ratios            | Liquidity buffer              | Derived                    | -                    | Monthly             | (Cash/TA)                                                        |
| ---               | ---                           | ---                        | ---                  | ---                 | ---                                                              |
| Ratios            | Working-capital ratio         | Derived                    | -                    | Monthly             | ((CA-CL)/TA)                                                     |
| ---               | ---                           | ---                        | ---                  | ---                 | ---                                                              |
| Ratios            | Profitability                 | Derived                    | -                    | Monthly             | (EBIT/TA)                                                        |
| ---               | ---                           | ---                        | ---                  | ---                 | ---                                                              |
| Market            | 1m / 3m / 6m return           | yfinance                   | Daily                | Monthly             | Rolling cumulative return ending at month-end                    |
| ---               | ---                           | ---                        | ---                  | ---                 | ---                                                              |
| Market            | 3m / 6m realized volatility   | yfinance                   | Daily                | Monthly             | Std. dev. of daily returns in rolling windows                    |
| ---               | ---                           | ---                        | ---                  | ---                 | ---                                                              |
| Market            | 12m backward drawdown         | yfinance                   | Daily                | Monthly             | Peak-to-trough over prior 252 trading days                       |
| ---               | ---                           | ---                        | ---                  | ---                 | ---                                                              |
| Market            | Idiosyncratic volatility      | Derived + market index     | Daily                | Monthly             | Residual volatility from rolling regression (Shumway motivation) |
| ---               | ---                           | ---                        | ---                  | ---                 | ---                                                              |
| Filing signals    | Late filing flag              | SEC Form 12b-25            | Event                | Monthly             | Indicator: any 12b-25 filing within last 6 months                |
| ---               | ---                           | ---                        | ---                  | ---                 | ---                                                              |
| Macro             | VIX                           | FRED VIXCLS                | Daily                | Monthly             | Month-avg or month-end VIX                                       |
| ---               | ---                           | ---                        | ---                  | ---                 | ---                                                              |
| Macro             | Term spread                   | FRED yields (10Y, 2Y)      | Daily                | Monthly             | Month-avg (10Y-2Y)                                               |
| ---               | ---                           | ---                        | ---                  | ---                 | ---                                                              |
| Macro             | Credit conditions proxy       | FRED corporate yield (BAA) | Daily/Monthly        | Monthly             | Month-avg BAA or spread proxy                                    |
| ---               | ---                           | ---                        | ---                  | ---                 | ---                                                              |
| Benchmark         | Altman Z-score                | Derived                    | Mixed                | Monthly             | Computed where inputs available; benchmark comparison            |
| ---               | ---                           | ---                        | ---                  | ---                 | ---                                                              |

**Data pipeline workflow diagram ![](data:image/png;base64,)**

**Methodology and evaluation strategy**

**Feature engineering and leakage control**

The feature engineering rules follow two core constraints.

First, **no look-ahead bias**: each firm-month feature vector (X\_{i,t}) must use only information available by month-end (t). SEC fundamentals are aligned using filing dates from submissions history and carried forward until the next filing, reflecting what analysts could know in real time.

Second, **mixed-frequency design**: fundamentals update quarterly/annually; market and macro update daily. Daily market and macro series are aggregated to month-end (or month averages), while fundamentals are carried forward between filings.

Data hygiene assumptions: ratio variables are winsorized within the training window, standardized using training parameters only, and missingness is handled with defensible rules (e.g., optional exclusion of interest-coverage if interest expense is missing broadly).

**Econometric models (interpretable)**

The modeling approach prioritizes clear probabilistic outputs and interpretable drivers.

**Pooled logistic regression (baseline).**  
Estimate ![](data:image/png;base64,) via logit across all firm-months (pooled). This specification is conceptually aligned with Ohlson's logit approach to predicting corporate failure.

**Fixed-effects panel logit (main).**  
Add industry fixed effects (SIC group) and month fixed effects to control for cross-industry structure and macro regimes. SIC use in EDGAR filings is justified by SEC's SIC description.

**Discrete-time hazard model via logit (dynamic EWS).**  
Build a risk-set panel where firms are included until their first deterioration event; estimate the conditional probability of event at (t) given survival until (t-1). This follows Shumway's argument that hazard models are more appropriate and can be estimated with logit programs.

**Optional structural proxy: distance-to-default.**  
If time permits, compute a simplified distance-to-default style measure motivated by Merton's structural model (equity as contingent claim on firm assets). This will be used as an additional covariate or a robustness benchmark, not as the primary model.

**Cross-sectional dependence and inference**

Because macro variables (e.g., VIX, interest rates) are common across firms in a given month, residuals may be correlated across firms within the same period. To avoid overstating statistical significance, inference will use **time-clustered standard errors** (cluster by month) for pooled/panel specifications, and robustness checks will use **month fixed effects** to absorb common shocks. SEC's emphasis on macro regimes motivates this practical econometric correction (assumption based on standard panel practice; included explicitly for rigor).

**Explainability outputs**

Interpretability is central. Primary explainability includes coefficient tables (odds ratios), marginal effects, and probability calibration (reliability curves, Brier score). Risk-decile monitoring reports event rates by predicted decile to translate model outputs into actionable "watchlist" logic.

SHAP is explicitly optional and not required; it will be considered only if a secondary non-linear benchmark is explored, but deep learning/black-box methods are avoided by design.

**Validation and evaluation**

**Time-based splits (primary).**  
Train: 2010-2020; validation: 2021-2023; test: 2024-2025 (assumption reflecting typical backtesting discipline).

**Rolling/expanding windows (robustness).**  
Expanding training window up to year (T) to predict year (T+1), repeated over multiple years.

**Metrics (early-warning focused).**  
Report AUROC, AUPRC (important under class imbalance), Brier score (calibration), top-K lift/capture (e.g., top 10% risk bucket), and lead time (months between first high-risk signal and event).

**Baseline comparisons (ablation).**  
Compare accounting-only, market-only, combined model, and Altman Z-score benchmark.

**Feasibility, risks, plan, and deliverables**

**Feasibility analysis**

**Data feasibility.**  
SEC EDGAR APIs provide submissions and extracted XBRL data as JSON, with public access and fair-access constraints; this supports a reproducible pipeline if rate limiting and caching are implemented.
FRED requires API keys but provides stable macro series access.
yfinance provides practical access to Yahoo Finance data but must be treated as a research-grade tool with documented legal disclaimers.
Cboe methodology offers primary justification of VIX meaning as a 30-day expected volatility measure.

**Computational needs.**  
With ~19k firm-months and a small feature set (<25), pooled/panel/hazard logit models run on standard laptops; the bottleneck is data engineering rather than computation.

**Tools (listed only; no code).**  
Python: pandas, numpy, requests/httpx, yfinance, fredapi, statsmodels, scikit-learn (metrics/calibration), matplotlib; optional: linearmodels (panel utilities), SQLite/parquet for caching.

**Risks, limitations, and mitigation**

**Yahoo Finance licensing/interface risk.**  
yfinance notes it is not affiliated with Yahoo and warns users to refer to Yahoo terms for rights to use downloaded data. Mitigation: cache downloaded data snapshots, record retrieval timestamps, do not redistribute raw data, and provide reproducible scripts.

**SEC rate limits/fair access.**  
SEC guidance caps automated requests and reserves rate controls. Mitigation: implement throttling, retries, and caching; keep sample ~60-80 firms.

**Label noise (market-implied deterioration).**  
Drawdown is a proxy for deterioration (not necessarily a rating downgrade). Mitigation: dual-label design including 8-K Item 1.03 bankruptcy benchmark for event validation and case studies.

**Class imbalance.**  
Bankruptcy label is rare. Mitigation: use AUPRC/top-K metrics; treat Label B as robustness; consider class-weighting in logit (assumption).

**Look-ahead bias.**  
Mitigation: align fundamentals by filing date and carry forward only after dissemination; audit timing on a subset.

**Timeline diagram**

**![](data:image/png;base64,)**

**Expected contributions**

**Academic contributions.**  
The project delivers an open-data, time-consistent early-warning design grounded in canonical literature, explicitly testing whether mixed-frequency market and fundamental signals improve deterioration forecasting under realistic time splits.

**Practical contributions.**  
The output is a reproducible monitoring scorecard (probabilities, deciles, lead-time) using public sources, with clear, communicable drivers suitable for credit analysts and risk staff.

**Final deliverables list**

The final deliverables consist of:

A reproducible repository (data ingestion, feature engineering, label construction, modeling, evaluation), a processed firm-month panel with data dictionary, interim report and slides, a public project webpage, the final MSc report with appendices (variable definitions, leakage controls, robustness checks), and oral examination presentation materials.
